elements.sent-2411-003: Learning a reward model (RM) from human preferences has been an important component in aligning large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-004: The canonical setup of learning RMs from pairwise preference data is rooted in the classic Bradley-Terry (BT) model that accepts binary feedback, i.e., the label being either Response 1 is better than Response 2, or the opposite.
elements.sent-2411-005: Such a setup inevitably discards potentially useful samples (such as ``tied'' between the two responses) and loses more fine-grained information (such as ``slightly better'').
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper, we propose a framework for learning RMs under ordinal feedback which generalizes the case of binary preference feedback to any arbitrary granularity.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, we first identify a marginal unbiasedness condition, which generalizes the assumption of the BT model in the existing binary feedback setting.
elements.sent-2411-008: The condition validates itself via the sociological concept of the wisdom of the crowd.
elements.sent-2411-009: Under the condition, we develop a natural probability model for pairwise preference data under ordinal feedback and analyze its properties.
elements.sent-2411-010: We prove the statistical benefits of ordinal feedback in terms of reducing the Rademacher complexity compared to the case of binary feedback.
elements.sent-2411-011: The proposed learning objective and the theory also extend to hinge loss and direct policy optimization (DPO).
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, the theoretical analysis may be of independent interest when applying to a seemingly unrelated problem of knowledge distillation to interpret the bias-variance trade-off therein.
elements.sent-2411-013: The framework also sheds light on writing guidance for human annotators.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our numerical experiments validate that fine-grained feedback leads to better reward learning for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.
elements.sent-2411-015: Further experiments show that incorporating a certain proportion of samples with tied preference boosts RM learning.
elements.sent-2411-016: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) \citep{christiano2017deep, ziegler2019fine, askell2021general, ouyang2022training is vital to aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The RLHF involves either explicitly training a reward model (RM) from human preferences data \citep{ouyang2022training or implicitly using the LLM itself as one \citep{rafailov2024direct .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, there is an inconsistency between current ways of collecting human preference data and the training of reward models.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, the Llama team collects fine-grained human feedback: they not only collect the preferred response but also 4 levels named ``significantly better'', ``better'', ``slightly better'', and ``marginally better'' \citep{llama2024llama , while the post-training of Llama 3 treats ``significantly better'' and ``better'' as the same and discard all the others.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Such a process wastes the potentially useful samples that cost the human annotators additional time and also it may loss the useful information hidden in the preference level.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we study the problem of reward modeling under ordinal feedback.
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, we relate the annotator's preference feedback with the probability that one response is better than the other on a population level.
elements.sent-2411-023: We introduce a marginal unbiasedness assumption as the only assumption that validates the probability setup of the ordinal feedback system.
elements.sent-2411-024: The assumption is rooted in the sociological concept of the wisdom of the crowd.
elements.sent-2411-025: Under the assumption, we analyze the properties of the probability model of ordinal feedback.
elements.sent-2411-026: We propose a learning objective for reward modeling with ordinal feedback, and the objective function naturally generalizes the case of binary feedback.
elements.sent-2411-027: Theoretically, we establish the advantage of ordinal feedback, which draws an interesting connection with the literature on soft labeling and knowledge distillation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our paper is organized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Section \ref{sec:setup , we model the general ordinal feedback by relating the feedback with the probability that a certain response is better than the other on a population level.}

elements.sent-2411-044: Consider the task of reward modeling based on the pairwise preference data.
elements.sent-2411-045: Each data sample consists of a tuple \begin{equation}(x, y_1, y_2, z)\end{equation} where $x\in \mathcal{X}$ denotes a prompt, $y_1, y_2\in \mathcal{Y}$ are two candidate responses to the prompt $x$, and $Z$ is a random variable (taking values in $\mathcal{Z}$) that denotes the feedback (generated by either human annotators or advanced AI models) indicating the preference between $y_1$ and $y_2$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The feedback $Z$ can be viewed as a proxy of the probability that $y_1$ is better than $y_2$ for the prompt $x$, denoted by $\mathbb{P (y_1 \succ y_2 | x)$.}

elements.sent-2411-048: The prevalent way to relate the reward model with the preference probability is via the Bradley-Terry model \citep{bradley1952rank \begin{equation}P(y_1 \succ y_2 | x) \approx \frac{\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big)}{\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big) + \exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_2)\big)}\end{equation} where we approximate the probability with the softmax reward values on the right-hand side.}

elements.sent-2411-216: The BTT model represents preference probabilities as follows: \begin{equation}P(y_1 \succ y_2 \mid x) \approx \frac{\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big)}{\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big) + \lambda \exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_2)\big)},\end{equation} \begin{equation}P(y_1 \sim y_2 \mid x) \approx \frac{(\lambda^2 - 1)\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big)\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_2)\big)}{\left(\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big) + \lambda \exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_2)\big)\right)\left(\lambda\exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_1)\big) + \exp\big(r_{\theta}(x, y_2)\big)\right)},\end{equation} where $y_1 \sim y_2$ denotes a tie between $y_1$ and $y_2$ and $\lambda \geq 1$ is a threshold hyperparameter that controls the likelihood of assigning ties.

elements.sent-2411-217: Notably, when $\lambda = 1$, the BTT model reduces to the canonical BT model.

elements.sent-2411-229: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) originates from the idea of preference-based reinforcement learning \citep{cheng2011preference, akrour2011preference .}
elements.sent-2411-230: The term RLHF is proposed by the large language model (LLM) community and has been a mainstream framework for aligning LLMs with human preferences \citep{askell2021general, ouyang2022training .}
elements.sent-2411-231: For a more detailed survey of the history of RLHF, we refer to \citet{kaufmann2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-232: While many people have incorporated the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage as a part of the RLHF pipeline \citep{ziegler2019fine, ouyang2022training, ji2023ai , our discussion would focus on the reward modeling and policy optimization via reinforcement learning (RL) which take a supervised fine-tuned model as the starting point.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Although proven effective for aligning LLMs with human preferences, the canonical RLHF suffers in several aspects, including complicated implementation, difficult hyperparameter tuning, low sampling efficiency \citep{choshen2019weaknesses and computational overhead \citep{yuan2024rrhf , which promotes the studies to optimize with the relative preferences without depending on RL.}}
elements.sent-2411-234: One major alternative to the RLHF is the direct policy optimization (DPO) proposed by \citet{rafailov2024direct , which directly trains the language model to increase the probability of the preferred response and decrease the other.}

elements.sent-2411-254: In this paper, we propose reward modeling with ordinal feedback as a generalization of the binary feedback.

elements.sent-2411-256: We generalize the assumption of the BT model to the general marginal unbiasedness assumption, which we name by a sociological concept ``wisdom of the crowd''.

elements.section-2411-008: Reward Modeling with Ordinal Feedback under Hinge Loss
elements.sent-2411-264: In the main paper, we focus on reward modeling with ordinal feedback under the cross-entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-265: Here we extend the analysis to the case of hinge loss.
elements.sent-2411-266: Recall that the learning objective \eqref{eqn:learning_objective in the main paper can be interpreted as an induction of the cross-entropy loss based on the Bradley-Terry model.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Given the probabilistic model of ordinal feedback, the learning objective can naturally be extended to incorporate other types of loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-268: Among these, hinge loss \citep{scholkopf2004kernel is one of the most widely used, particularly in classification tasks, alongside cross-entropy loss.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Hinge loss is commonly associated with Support Vector Machines (SVM) and is characterized by its core principle of enforcing a margin between distinct classes.
elements.sent-2411-270: Building on this observation, we define the learning objective under hinge loss as follows: \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} \ \sum_{i=1}^n Z_{i}\cdot \left[ \max\left( 0, C-\left(r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,1}) - r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,2})\right) \right) \right] + (1-Z_{i})\cdot \left[ \max\left( 0, C-\left(r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,2}) - r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,1})\right) \right) \right], \label{eqn:hinge_objective}\end{equation} where $C$ is the margin hyperparameter that controls the separation between preference classes.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} \ \sum_{i=1}^n Z_{i}\cdot \left[ \max\left( 0, C-\left(r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,1}) - r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,2})\right) \right) \right] + (1-Z_{i})\cdot \left[ \max\left( 0, C-\left(r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,2}) - r_{\theta}(x_i, y_{i,1})\right) \right) \right], \label{eqn:hinge_objective}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-271: When the feedback is binary, i.e., $Z_i \in \{0, 1\}$, the above objective simplifies to the hinge loss commonly used in reward modeling \citep{liu2024skywork .}
elements.sent-2411-272: In our experiments, the margin parameter is tuned with grid search, where the search space is $\{0.5, 1, 2, 4\}$, and we select $C=2$.
elements.sent-2411-273: To further validate the conclusions presented in Section \ref{sec:experiments , we conduct analogous experiments under the learning objective \eqref{eqn:hinge_objective .}}

elements.sent-2411-276: We then replicate the experimental setup from Section \ref{subsec:HE_experiment , employing llama-3.2-1b-instruct as our base model.}
elements.sent-2411-277: The experiment results are reported in Figure \ref{fig:GH_experiment_results and Table \ref{tab:GH_experiment_results .}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Experiments illustrate the same three conclusions in Section \ref{subsec:HE_experiment : (i) more fine-grained feedback structures result in better learning for both ID and OOD performance; (ii) the fine-grained feedback (e.g. 5-level) may be a good proxy for the oracle; (iii) the generalized hinge loss handle the feedback richer than binary well.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Apart from the observations analogous to Section \ref{subsec:HE_experiment , we observe that the hinge objective performs weaker than the cross-entropy objective.}
elements.sent-2411-280: We attribute this outcome to two key factors.
elements.sent-2411-281: First, the margin hyperparameter $C$ significantly impacts the model's convergence speed and overall performance, which we only tune with a coarse grid due to computational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-282: Second, the inherent nature of the hinge objective means that only a specific subset of data points influences the final decision boundary.
elements.sent-2411-283: In contrast, the cross-entropy objective leverages the entire dataset during optimization.
elements.sent-2411-284: Given the complexity of language modeling and the intricacies of semantic space, we hypothesize that preference data embeddings are distributed in a noisy and overlapping manner such that the decision boundary may not be effectively established under the hinge objective.

elements.sent-2411-317: Cross-entropy loss: The cross-entropy loss is \begin{equation}\ell_{ce}(Z, z) = -\left[Z \log(z) + (1-Z) \log(1-z)\right],\end{equation} where $z \in [0, 1]$, which is the probability under the BT model.
elements.sent-2411-318: The cross-entropy loss is affine to $Z$: \allowdisplaybreaks \begin{equation}E_{Z}\big[\ell_{ce}(Z, z)\big] = -\sum_{j, z_j \in Z} P(Z = z_j) \cdot \left[z_j \cdot \log(z) + (1-z_j) \cdot \log(1-z)\right] = -\left[\Big(\sum_{j, z_j \in Z} P(Z = z_j) \cdot z_j\Big) \cdot \log(z) + \bigg(1 - \Big(\sum_{j, z_j \in Z} P(Z = z_j) \cdot z_j\Big)\bigg) \cdot \log(1-z)\right] = -\left[E[Z] \cdot \log(z) + \big(1-E[Z]\big) \cdot \log(1-z)\right] = \ell_{ce}\big(E[Z], z\big).\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{3-level Sampling Algorithm} \label{alg:sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $z_{\text{oracle}} \in [0, 1]$ \Comment{Oracle label} \Ensure $z \in \mathcal{Z}_3 = \{0, 0.5, 1\}$ \Comment{Sampled label} \If{$z_{\text{oracle}} < 0.5$} \State Sample $y \sim \text{Bernoulli}\left(\frac{z_{\text{oracle}}}{0.5}\right)$ \State $z \gets 0.5 \cdot y$ \ElsIf{$z_{\text{oracle}} > 0.5$} \State Sample $y \sim \text{Bernoulli}\left(\frac{z_{\text{oracle}} - 0.5}{0.5}\right)$ \State $z \gets 0.5 \cdot y + 0.5$ \Else \State $z \gets 0.5$ \EndIf \State \Return $z$ \end{algorithmic}
